content: Hi! In my work on the rust vkms driver, I came across another thing it looks like I would need to add rust support for in the kernel: iosys_map bindings. I'm not entirely sure what these would look like yet, but my assumption is because of how general this interface is that it might be a good idea to make it !Send + !Sync, and expose IO operations as unsafe methods so that users implement their own safe abstractions around it to fit their needs. Does this seem like a good plan?

(disclamer, I don't know what iosys_map is and have just read the doc overview in iosys-map.h)

Do you mind sharing what you need to use iosys_map for? What makes you think that we cannot provide safe abstractions?

Benno Lossin said:

(disclamer, I don't know what iosys_map is and have just read the doc overview in iosys-map.h)

Do you mind sharing what you need to use iosys_map for? What makes you think that we cannot provide safe abstractions?

The main reason I want to use it is so that we can handle return values from DRM functions like drm_gem_fb_vmap() (drivers/gpu/drm/drm_gem_framebuffer_helper.c) for mapping framebuffers that could live in either system memory or IO memory at runtime.

Also - I actually guess maybe we could expose them as safe? I had assumed this was something that would be an unsafe operation because a generic type for a memory mapping isn't really going to know what access rules ithe memory is governed by. However, it seems like the kernel::io::Io type we currently have in the kernel actually exposes stuff like readw/readl/etc. as safe methods so I guess perhaps safe would be fine here?

Going to start working on this today - I'm thinking of trying to handle this similarly to io.rs, except we use a struct like this:

pub struct IosysMap<const SIZE: usize = 0> {
  map: bindings::iosys_map,
  maxsize: usize,
}

And probably require that any conversions from a raw iosys_map require that the user specify the size to store in maxsize

Though I've been wondering if we should maybe do some sort of a conversion from iosys_map to a rust type instead of embedding the struct. It feels like it's a bit of a shame to have something redundant like this when we already have io.rs
(I just realized io.rs isn't actually upstream yet, whoops!)

Could we also do this?:

/// # Invariants
/// - the pointer stored in `map` points to a valid `T`
pub struct IoSysMap<T> {
    map: Opaque<bindings::iosys_map>,
    _p: PhantomData<T>,
}

And then you eg could use IoSysMap<[u8; 256]>. Some more questions:

  • Do you need to have a variable size? Or do you know it statically?
  • what do these normally point to just opaque data (ie [u8]) or do you also store structs with eg mutexes or similar stuff?

in case you don't know the size statically (which I think is the case, based on your approach), then the idea above doesn't work.

Lyude said:

(I just realized io.rs isn't actually upstream yet, whoops!)

is it on the rust branch? I only found io_{buffer,mem,iter}.rs there.

Benno Lossin said:

  • Do you need to have a variable size? Or do you know it statically?

honestly I don't and TBH I'd rather it not, I was mostly just copying what @Danilo Krummrich has been doing. Supposedly the maxsize thing is for when we don't know the size of something statically.

Benno Lossin said:

  • what do these normally point to just opaque data (ie [u8]) or do you also store structs with eg mutexes or similar stuff?

This is a very general interface used all over the kernel, so it can really point to anything. Sometimes it might be a struct, in my case it is simply a framebuffer - so a linear array of pixel data. That being said - I don't think any non-primitive types would ever be stored in them

Benno Lossin said:

Lyude said:

(I just realized io.rs isn't actually upstream yet, whoops!)

is it on the rust branch? I only found io_{buffer,mem,iter}.rs there.

I will have to check, it might be on the nova branch actually

@Benno Lossin You can find it in [1] and it's used by the PCI abstractions in [2].

We can probably reuse it for an abstraction for iosys_map, but I still need to have a look on that.

@Lyude I did not get to it yet, but tomorrow I should have some time for that.

[1] https://github.com/Rust-for-Linux/linux/commits/staging/rust-device/
[2] https://github.com/Rust-for-Linux/linux/commits/staging/rust-pci/

Lyude said:

Benno Lossin said:

  • Do you need to have a variable size? Or do you know it statically?

honestly I don't and TBH I'd rather it not, I was mostly just copying what Danilo Krummrich has been doing. Supposedly the maxsize thing is for when we don't know the size of something statically.

sorry, I am confused, you don't need and don't want to have variable size or you don't know it statically?

Benno Lossin said:

  • what do these normally point to just opaque data (ie [u8]) or do you also store structs with eg mutexes or similar stuff?

This is a very general interface used all over the kernel, so it can really point to anything. Sometimes it might be a struct, in my case it is simply a framebuffer - so a linear array of pixel data. That being said - I don't think any non-primitive types would ever be stored in them

I see, but it still would be useful to be able to give the type of the pointed to value, so I think we should support that.

Benno Lossin said:

I see, but it still would be useful to be able to give the type of the pointed to value, so I think we should support that.

Oh yeah - this was kind of why I was hoping to mimic the way that dakr's interface currently works, since they've got support for reading a number of primitives
